title: Constant Path Length
concept: In RNNs, signals fade over N steps; in Transformers, any two words interact in one step, resolving vanishing gradients across long texts.

exercises.prompt: What happens to the signal in an RNN as the distance between two related words increases?

options.text: The signal fades and gets weaker.
The signal stays exactly the same.

exercises.explain: As sentences get longer, the chain of steps in an RNN grows, making it harder for the model to link words at the beginning and the end.

cards.title: The Memory Fade
cards.body: Have you ever read the end of a long sentence and completely forgotten how it started? Traditional AI brains (called RNNs) suffer from the same problem. Let's find out how modern AIs solved this memory loss!

cards.title: What is Constant Path Length?
cards.body: In deep learning, path length is the number of steps a signal must travel to connect two words. RNNs have a variable path length that grows with text length, causing signals to fade. Transformers have a constant path length of exactly one step, keeping the signal perfectly clear.

cards.title: The Whisper Game vs. Zoom
cards.body: Imagine passing a secret down a line of 50 kids. By the last kid, the message is completely scrambled. That is an RNN. Now, imagine a giant Zoom call where everyone can instantly whisper directly to anyone else in one second. That is a Transformer!

cards.title: Direct Connections in Action
cards.body: In the sentence "The **dog**, which chased three cats, two squirrels, and ran through the mud, was **wet**," an RNN must carry the word "dog" through 12 steps to connect it to "wet." A Transformer connects "dog" and "wet" directly in one single step.

cards.title: The Infinite Memory Myth
cards.body: People think Transformers remember everything because they have infinite memory. Actually, their memory isn't infinite—they are just designed so that every word is always exactly one step away from every other word, preventing information from getting lost.

cards.title: Path Length Comparison
cards.body: See how the route of information changes between these two AI architectures.

left.text: Signals must pass through every word in sequence. Long paths cause the signal to fade away.

right.text: Every word has a direct, one-step link to every other word. The signal stays strong.

exercises.prompt: Why do older RNN models struggle to remember connections in very long sentences?

options.text: The signal fades as it travels step-by-step through a long chain of words.
They can only read the first three words of any sentence.
They automatically delete long words to save space.
They do not support English grammar rules.

exercises.explain: RNNs process words sequentially, meaning a signal must travel through every word in between, causing the information to fade (the vanishing gradient problem).

exercises.prompt: True or False: In a Transformer, the distance (path length) between the first word and the last word increases as the sentence gets longer.
exercises.explain: In a Transformer, the path length between any two words is always exactly one step, regardless of how far apart they are in the sentence.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how quickly words connect in a Transformer.
exercises.sentence: While RNNs take many steps to connect distant words, Transformers do it in just ___ step.
exercises.explain: No matter how long the text is, a Transformer connects any two words in exactly one step.

exercises.prompt: An AI helper says: "Transformers prevent vanishing gradients because the path length between any two words is always a constant value of one step." Is the AI helper correct?

options.text: No, the path length increases with sentence length.
No, Transformers have a path length of zero.

exercises.explain: The AI system is correct. A constant path length of one means the distance between any two words is always a single step, preventing signals from decaying over time.

exercises.prompt: How do Transformers prevent signals from fading over long texts compared to older RNN networks?

options.text: They force the network to process words one by one much faster.
They allow any two words to connect in a single step, bypassing long chains.
They delete all middle words to shorten the sentence.
They use extra memory to repeat the first word at every step.

exercises.explain: Transformers solve the vanishing gradient problem by allowing any two words in a text to connect directly in exactly one step, keeping the path length constant no matter how long the document is.
